a- ] (N.K.) ma-, conc. prefix (U), n., a., & p.c. cl, 2.

a / ma, interrog. pron. (U), ref. to cL 2, who (exactly) ?, which people?; nĩ ~ moka?, nĩ ~ na ~?, ni na tũhi, who came?, who and who?, the little girls and boys; nĩ inyur ~?, inyu nĩ ~?, who are you? ; nĩ ~ aya!, shame on you, what sort of people do you think you are!

-a, part. (U), indic. relationship, association, or possession, preceded by the p.c. of all classes; the meaning is expr. by var. prep. as ’ well as the poss. in English ; nguo ci~ Kamau, K’s clothes; njira y~ rũ, the path to the river; mũgũnda w~ kĩanda, a garden in a valley; cira w~ gĩthaka, a case about land; akĩmũtheca rĩ~ nda, he stabbed him with a spear in the stomach; nĩ h~ rũĩmbo, it is a dancing place; see also ha; nĩ kw~ riũa mũno, it is a very hot country; see also kwa.

-a-, v. infix (U), sign of the immediate past tenses.

a’ | aa [aa-a], interj. (U), expr. deep disgust, irritation; ~, thi na kũ, Oh, for heaven’s sake, go away !

aa, pers. collective part. (U), those with, those belonging to, the associates of, the crowd with; twarĩra ~ Kamau, take it to K. and the people with him; nao ~ Njeri N. and her crowd went home; ~ baba, father and his brothers, his initiation mates, father and anyone who happens to be with him at the time; ~ Macaria, M. and his companions; see -a.

aana, interj. (U), (N.K.) expr. disapproval, refusal to believe often accompanied by gesture of erect forefinger in face of person addressed (usually only a younger person).

aari, see naari.

aba / abai!, interj. (U), expr. disapproval of an action, or warning; ~ rĩrĩa baba arĩũka niũrĩmenya!, my ! when father comes you will get it thick!; ~ ndikwĩrire!, I told you so, clever-Dick!; cf. wee.

abababa-ĩ [aabababaa-ĩj, interj. (U), expr. surprise, consternation, or mild annoyance; ~, mbembe ici ti njiarul, well, well, this maize isn’t bearing yet; cf. a’.

Abaci, see Habaci.

abai?, n. cl. 1/2 (111), used in ref. to one’s father, or addressed familiarly to another male; ~ atarĩ ho gũtiri kĩngiuma mũcii ny, in Daddd’s absence nothing shall be taken from this homestead; ~ nĩtũthii, (said even to a boy companion) come on, Dad, let’s be off!

abaita / aboita, interj. (U), expr. surprise coupled with indignation.

abando / abondo / abandothi / abondothic’, interj. (U), an obscene cursing expression; cf. abaita, aba.

abo, interj. (U), 1. used by women in conjunction with names, praise names associated with various clans and the word ‘baba’ (q.v.), as a mild form of oath or to express surprise, consternation, or indignation; ~ Angarĩ aitũ, kaĩ ndi ngombo!, good heavens, by the community of my clan Angari, am I a slave?; ~ baba, by my father, I will not go! 2. used with the foll. forms of clan names: Angari, ~ Anjirũ, ~ Njirũ;, Aceera, ~ Njeeri; Agacikũ, ~ Ambũi, ~ Mbũi; Ethaga, ~ Mbura; Aithirandũ, ~ Tthĩra; Angũi, ~ Ngũi; Aicakamũyũ, ~ ~ Iriimũ.

aboita, see abaita.

aca [aaca], part. (U), (expr. negation) no; ~ noguo (nĩguo), indeed there is no doubt about it; ~-ni, (said to more than one person) certainly not!; cf. arĩ, naari.

rũacũ, n. (11), 1. chip, shiver, splinter; cf. rokaracũ, rũcacũ. 2. thin wand as used for partition; see 3. long piece of split sugarcane suitable for children; cf. rabũa. [see acũka]

acũka, v.i. (I1), (of foodstuffs) be just right, be in good condition for eating, be crisp, ripe, mellow, &c.; (of sugar-cane) be succulent, crisp, easily broken, ripe; (of beverages) taste well, be mellow; (of bread) be light, wel! cooked; (of soil) be dry, friable, in good condition for digging; cf. ondoka. acaacaka, dim. acũkanga, diff. acũgkanĩra, assoc. acũkia, caus.; kabura kau nĩko gaacũkĩtie igwa, that light rain has mellowed the sugar-cane. acũra, v.t. 1. make just right; make crisp, ripe, mellow; arĩa maanj~ ga, those who were giving me pleasure. 2., make (soil) dry, cracked.

-ag-!, v. infix, indic. the habitual aspect of an action in past, present, or future time ; nĩmaathom~a, they were regular students (in the past).

-ag-?, v. root extension, indic. an action that takes place while sth. else is happening; rut~a wĩra, get on with your work, we are going to leave you; thi~a, go on, I will follow.

aga, v.t. & i. (11), 1. lack, miss, fail to find; be in need of; be missing; ~ mwĩkĩre, be at a loss to know what to do; ~ tha (bata, kĩene, rũbutu), see under tha, &c.; ~ mat, see under matũ ; ~ thoni see under thoni; ~ mũndũ, give no consideration to sb. ; ignore, treat with contempt. 2. (used as aux. v. in neg. sentences) kw~ kuura irio itikũra, if it does not rain, the crops will not grow; kw~ Ũguo failing this I shall not go; cf. rega, tiga. agaaga, dim. aganga, diff. agana, rec. miss one another, fail to meet; treat each other contemptuously., Jtina na thĩ iti~ga, the buttocks and the ground cannot miss one another for ever (i.e. one is bound to come to rest some time). aganira, rec. app. agia, caus. make miss, cause not to have or find sth., deprive sth.; ~ gĩkaro, cause not to have an abode, rest, peace. fagia, caus. refl. deprive oneself of; ~ hinya, wear oneself out. agithia, d. caus.  cause sb. to lack or miss sth., deprive sb. of sth.; ~ mũndũ gĩtĩo, cause a person to miss esteem, belittle. agĩka, st. pot. agĩra?, app. fail to find for; be without sth. for or at. refi. app.; ~ bata, feel worthless.

(v), (only used in compound nouns) one who lacks: x. ~-hinya, weak, powerless, person. 2. ~-irĩ, (term of abuse) one without possessions or progeny; cf. ni-iri. 3. ~-thoni, one who lacks modesty, ill-mannered, disrespectful, person; cf. mũra-thoni. 4. ~-tha, one without compassion, humane feelings, pity. waga, n. cl. 14 (only in compounds): 1. ~-hinya, weakness. 2. ~-thoni, shamelessness, immodesty, rudeness, lack of manners; cf. riko. 3. ~-tha, mercilessness, callousness. (1), be restless, agitated, harassed, unsettled, perturbed; vacillate, move to and fro, oscillate, bob up and down, wobble about; ~ (ta maĩ ma irũtũ-inĩ), be extremely restless (as wateron an arum leaf); cf. hũga. agaagaga, dim. agaganga, diff. agagania, caus, rec. make each other, others, restless, perturbed, bewildered, &c. agaganĩra, assoc, agagia, caus. agitate, make perturbed or restless; hurry, harass; shake (liquids); gargle; swill out (containers, utensils); ~ ikombe maĩ, rinse out cups with water; ~ maĩ kanua, gargle water in the mouth; ~ kanua, indulge in futile and wild talk; ~ kũhũri-ini, swill gruel round in the half-calabash (before drinking or pouring out to counteract the effects of settlement); tiga kũnj~, don’t harass me. agagithia, d. caus. cause restlessness, embarrassment, homelessness, agagika, st. pot. app. vacillate in relation to; be restless, at, because of; become too much for, make perturbed, flustered ; cf. gagaĩra. agagirũo, pass. app.; ~ nĩ thĩ, be at a loss to know what to do or where to go. agagiria, caus. app. rinse out at; shake, make restless, agitate, in, at, for.

mwaka, mi-, n, (111), 1. year; ~ wa mĩrongo ĩrĩ, yo920. 2. season; the names of the main divisions and their order vary according to altitude and local vagaries of climate; those set out here follow approximately chronological order where there are two growing seasons; see dictionary entries. 1. early part of the year: mũgaa, kagaa, kĩrnjangwe, mwaniathenge. 2. March to May: mbura-yanjahĩ, mũringo, kĩhu, gĩthananwa, mũgira-njara. 3. June to August: gathaatn, muoria-nyoni, gaciciũ, gathano, (N.K.) gathano-komũ. 4. September to December: mbura-ya-mwere, mũringo, drera-hũngũ, kanya-hũngũ, kĩya-kia-mũhĩa (mwere), kayakarongo, kĩhu, gĩthananwa, mũratho, njakoira, thano, themithũ, gatumu (tumu); cf. kĩmera, hĩndĩ, kahinda.

mwakano, mi-, n. (1), blaze, flare up, heat; anger, indignation; severe pain. njakano, ~, n. I. sharp pain (less severe than above). 2. (coll.) poking fun at sb. or one another; ‘sitting on’, attacking, insinuating things about, one another. [akia]

-ake, p.a. (U), poss. a. & pron. his; her, hers.

mwakit, a-, n. (111), builder, mason. [aka]

mwaki? mĩ-, n. (1), 1. fire; 7kĩra horo ~, make sth. more urgent or arresting; ota ~, see under ota, cf. thiota; rra ~, enjoy oneself, be out for a spree; nicknamed (N.K.) mwarĩ-wandune. 2. a territorial unit of several villages; a ~ ũmwe, people of a fire-linked unit; krama kĩa ~, see under kĩama. [see akia]

akia, v.t, (1), light (a fire, lamp, &c.), turn on (electric light); cf. mundia, gwatia. akangia, diff. akana, rec. burn, light up, be lighted; (of lights) be on, shine, akanwoo, pass. rec.;

krama', ci-, n. (1), those set apart as a council, council of elders; council, tribunal, association, party, committee; a deliberative, advisory, legislative, judicial, or executive body in a country, province, district, ridge, or village; the graduated system of eldership for which Kikuyu married men become eligible; ~ ga kamatimũ, the first grade of elders composed of recently married men with at least one child (admitted on payment of a goat or sheep, and carrying spears instead of staves of offce, who serve as assistants in the court as part of their training but are not entitled to judge a case, though they provide the core of the army); see njama ya ita; cf. (N.K.) ~ kĩa mbiri imwe, ~ kĩrĩa kĩnyinyi, ~ kĩa lower degree of the ‘kiama’ proper (carrying unblackened staff and leaves loosely tied); ~ kĩa matathi, also ~ kĩrĩa ~ gĩa athamaki, ~ kĩrĩa gĩkũrũ, ~ kĩa njoho, ~ kĩa bũrũri, (Ndia) ~ gra ituĩro, ‘kiama’ proper, council of senior elders (the highest territorial body with judicial and administrative functions, composed of those who possess initiated children, carrying a blackened staff, as symbol of office see matathi); ~ kĩa maturanguru, the inner circle of the council of senior elders with the highest religious and political authority, carrying ‘maturangurv’ (q.v.) and wearing ‘icũh? (q.v.), also (N.K.) ~ gia kĩrogo (because of their power to put a curse on any one who persists in defying their decisions or orders); ~ kĩa ihiga, tribunal court, tribunal; ~ gĩa atumia, women’s guild; ~ kĩa Barũthi (Ngabana), Governor’s Executive Council, also Legislative Council; ~ kĩa mbũkĩra, an emergency council; ~ kĩa mũbira, football league; ~ kĩa ndundu, secret or private council, confidential committee, inner circle; ~ gĩa village council; ~ kĩa rũgongo, ridge or location council; ~ kĩa mwaki, council of a firelinked unit or group of villages; ~ kĩa bũrũri, inter-district or country-wide council; ~ kĩa a political party or association; ~ kĩa aruti a wĩra, trade union; ~ kĩa endia a irio, foodsellers’ co-operative society; ~ gĩa 2 kanitha, the governing body of a church. mũkĩama, a-, n, member of the council of elders; member of any council or committee,

maambura, n. cl, 6 (1), 1. ceremonial accompanying certain ‘rites de passage’; sacred rituals (including coition between the father and the mother of the homestead concerned) connected with the normal events of family life, birth, second birth, puberty, initiation, circumcision, marriage, performed under the direction of senior elders or old women, supplemented at circumcision rites by the mnd mũgo wa ~; ruta ~, perform the rituals. mwendia-rũhiũ, mĩ-, n. (I-11), 1. man who contracts a matrilocal, matrilineal marriage; one who cannot pay customary ‘bride-price? through poverty but attaches himself to the family of a man with many daughters, one of whom he is allowed to treat as wife (he remains there and works for his father-in-law, the children belong to their maternal grandfather and his lineage, the eldest son being named after a maternal grandparent. Such marriage is always convertible into the normal patrilocal, patrilineal form by subsequent payment of bride-price). 2. unattached man who cohabits with a widow or, by permission, with a married woman. 3. one who helps a woman to clear herself of ‘uncleanness’ due to the death of her husband by having sexual intercourse with her, unless this is done by the younger brother who inherits her; cf. mũthambania. 4. stranger willing to perform ceremonial coition with a woman on one of the occasions prescribed by custom, e.g. if she bears twins or has a child which cuts the upper teeth first, or, in the case of a newly initiated girl, at the conclusion of the aggregation rites; see mbiro ya rũenji.

(111), 1. tall timbertree (Rapanea rhododendroides). 2. bush (Myrsine africana). ngaita, ~, n. fruit of Myrsine africana (purple in colour, turning to light brown when fully ripe) used in broth as a general stomach medicine; mbũri, thenge, ya ~, goat, ram, eaten after an illness, providing broth in which the fruit has been boiled; rra ta ~, be extremely bitter, be vicious.

(11), pant; be, get, out of breath; ~ ta kanyoni, become completely breathless. hnũmahũma, dim. hũmahũmũka, neut. dim. rest a little bit. hũmanga, diff. caus. rec. compete, try to out-do one another (in running, drinking, talking, &c.). hũmanĩra, assoc. hũmaniria, caus. assoc., hũmia, caus. make pant, make lose breath (make it difficult for the ones behind to catch up). hũmithia, d. caus. hũmika, st. pot. hũmira, app. hũmirĩra, inten. 1. pant very fast. 2. speak with laboured breath. hbhũmũra, conv. ; ~ thoguo mũrigo release your father from that task. hũmũka, neut. 1. get one’s breath back; draw a deep breath, heave a sigh. 2. make a pause in a journey for drinking, &c. hũmũkanga, diff. neut. hũmũkania, caus. rec. make people rest. hũmũkanira, assoc. hũmũkanĩria, caus. assoc. neut. hũmũkia, caus, neut. make rest; ~ mũrigo, put down a load and rest. d. caus. neut. haũmũkĩra, app. neut. caus. app. neut. take to rest (for sb.). ~, n. hwa, ideo. (U), descr. of: 1. smooth, swift movement; athiaga ~ ~, he walks with long, swinging strides. 2. a flying leap; ndarnũnũga o ~ na ndihutie kagiri, I jumped clean over the hedge without touching it at all. 3. cutting through with a sharp knife.

hwaa, ideo. (u), descr. of: 1. surprise or effect of pleasure. 2. smooth motion, e.g. a gush of diarrhoea, of pus from a boil, the quiet easy movement of a motor-car, people moving along at a smooth even pace; nyua o ~, drink it down in one unbroken draught.

Mau-mau?, n. (1), name given to a reactionary organization among the Kikuyu, anti-European, anti-Christian, which after working covertly for a time established its influence over the majority of the people; after difficult and prolonged operations by the Government its gangs were reduced and dissipated, and by the end of 1956 only a handful remained at large; the name is said to have been first applied towards the end of the nineteenth century to a band of young men who terrorized a locality in the Kiambu district; see mau-mau’,

(111), sheep, female sheep, ewe (most relied on for sacrificial purposes; though a goat is used on occasion its virtue resides in the fact that it represents a sheep; if it is necessary to use a goat for a ceremony a female is given a piece of sheep’s fat to eat; failing this the medicine-man may employ a plant for lustration purposes or a dried hyrax’ stomach; see kahu ga gĩtoore, gĩkami); ~ ya ago, a plant planted by medicine-men and used by them for ritual cleansing purposes when a client cannot produce a live sheep or goat; kaka ~, see haka; ~l, (as term of abuse) idiot! ~ yũragaga (even) a sheep can break a cooking-pot. Gũtire ũru (thahu) Ũtarĩ ~, there is no evil (causing ceremonial pollution} which has not got its ritual cure (by means of the potent virtue of the sheep). kĩgondu, i-, n. half-grown lamb, young ewe; cf. gitũrũme. kagondu, tũ-, n. female lamb; young, small, sheep; cf. gatũrũme.

(b) ~ ya thĩ, general term for snake; cf. nyoka. (c) ~ ya rĩera-inĩ, bird of the air, cf. nyoni; ~ ya igũrũ, any large highflying bird, bird of prey. (d) ~-yaheho, (N.K.) colobus monkey. (e) ~-yambũri, (N.K.) leopard. (f) ~-cia-ũtukũ, safari ants (used at night to avoid referring to thuraku q.v.). 3. thing, object, anything not readily nameable or which one does not wish to name (esp. ref. to an object in cl. 9/10.) 4. a man’s name (given to a new-born child after a number of children have died in the family, in order to change the run of bad luck and in the hope that the angry departed spirit would ignore this one). mũnyamg, mi-, n stuff, material, substance. mũriira, mi-, n. (1), sp. of creeper or trailer vine, used as follows: 1. to make ornamental neck or head bands consisting of a series of small loops on a narrow thong; see (N.K.) tranga, ikuno, ĩthuno. 2. for tying up a goat for the oath-taking ceremony and the corners of a garment at the shoulder during the death-cleansing rites; see hukũra, ringa 3. in erection of magic arch at entrance to homestead in which initiation ceremonies are taking place, together with trunk and leaves of male bananas and castor-oil, to prevent harmful influences from entering the village, spoiling rites and harming neophytes. 4. for tying meat; cf. thũrũra, motamba. rũriira, ndiira, n. fibre from the above creeper.

iruta | rĩruta, ma-, n. mũtuithania!, a-, n, (1v), arbitrator, one who decides between two disputants. [tua!]

maũtuithania?, a-, n. (1v), one who severs, disunites. [tua]

ituika, (ma-), n. (111), a handing over or ceremonial transfer of responsibility from one generation-set to another; in addition to the annual circumcision or initiatory age-sets, (see marika ma kahiũ) there were generation-sets (also called marika) each covering a period of thirty years or more, during which the duly qualified representatives were in authority and responsible for the conduct of public affairs; the transfer of responsibility involved the contribution of a goat or sheep from every accountable member of the incoming generation towards the redemption of the country from the outgoing set (see kũra bũrũri); this process took many years. The best remembered of these ruling generation-sets, in receding order, are: Mwangi, Maina, Iregi, Ndemi, Mathathi, Cĩra, Cuma. The ‘Maina’ generation handed over to the ‘Mwang? about 1900; the latest, the ‘Trũngũ’ or ‘Maina Kanyi’ set, which is said to have been preparing to succeed the ‘Mwangi’ sometime between 1925-32, was prevented by the social and political changes brought about by europeanizing influences and by prohibition of the ceremonies by Government. [tua1]

gĩtuĩkano, i-, n. (111), separation, breaking up of herds; break-up of a fellowship, companionship; ~ kĩao kĩonekire o maarna, the breach between them became apparent when they fought. [tua']

-u, p.a. (U), stem of dem. a. & p., cl. 5, (6), 7» II, I2, 13, I5, and 16, that, those, (near the person addressed, near or in sth., previously mentioned). With the prefixes of cl. 5, 15b, and 16 there are special meanings: 1. cl. 5, rĩ~, now; o rĩ~, here and now, just a few moments ago; o na rĩ~, even now, certainly; kĩrĩ~, see sep. entry kĩriu. 2. cl. 15b; ki~, there; thit na kũ~, get away with you; gũtiri mũndũ, there is nobody there; gĩkn~, after the manner of that place, local. 3. cl. 16, ha~, just there, thereabouts; thĩ na ha~, go over there, in that direction; kĩrĩ ha~, (an evasive reply, when repeatedly asked where sth. is, to close the matter) it is somewhere there; one nĩ ha~ haranduura, that’s where I feel the pain.

muuma, mi-, n. (11), solemn oath, administered with due ritual before a tribunal of elders in daytime and in public (not to a woman or child); rro nĩ ~, be harmed or destroyed by an oath one has taken; nyua, ringa ~, take a solemn oath, cf. ringa gĩthathi, thenge; nyua ~ na kĩreru, take an oath insincerely, in pretence; ~ muumũkũ, (in a jocular sense) an ineffectual oath, one that has ceased to be binding or to have any power; cf. mwĩhĩtwa. w-, may represent -the concord prefix of cl. 14, when foll. by a vowel; in such cases the nouns must be looked for as usual under the initial letter of the stem.

wa / waa, interj. (U), expr. surprise, amazement, mockery, derision.

-wa, suffix (U), contracted form of poss. a. ref. to Ist pers., used with a few n. of cl. 1/2, written conjunctively; my relative; see -akwa.

-wa, part. (U), invariable, preceded by ni, no, ti, to written conjunctively, foll. by demonstratives of all classes, only at the beginning of a sentence, often indicating a sense of surprise or relief after a period of anticipation; nĩ~ gĩkĩ, this is it (what we have been looking for).

wa-, an invariable extra prefix found with certain words esp. the names of animals in stories often associated with cl. 1/2 concords; it may be followed by the normal class prefix.
